Mercedes E‑Class V6 Petrol Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E‑Class E280 / E320 / E350 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E‑Class V6 petrol engine typically costs £1,800–£4,200 supply only depending on engine generation (M112, M272 or M276). A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,500. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E‑Class E280 / E320 / E350 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine your E‑Class has. The M112 (1998‑2005) is the most robust of the three – it 'proved itself' and is regarded as a solid, reliable engine. The M272 (2005‑2011) is a more modern engine but suffers from a well‑documented balance shaft gear failure on early engines – check the engine serial number. The M276 (2011‑2016) is refined and powerful but can suffer from camshaft adjuster wear.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E‑Class E280 / E320 / E350 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the M112 (E280/E320, 1998‑2005), the main issue is valve cover oil leaks. On the M272 (E280/E350, 2005‑2011), the defining weakness is balance shaft gear failure – one of the most common and expensive Mercedes engine repairs on 2004‑2011 models. On the M276 (E350, 2011‑2016), camshaft adjuster wear is the primary concern.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E‑Class V6 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a tidy £3,000–£8,000 E‑Class, a £1,800–£4,200 recon is 20‑60% of the car's value – often worth it on well‑maintained examples. If the M272 balance shaft has failed catastrophically, a full engine replacement may be necessary. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing. The W211 E‑Class is becoming a modern classic, so well‑preserved examples may justify the investment.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E‑Class's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W210 (1998‑2002) E280 uses the M112.921 (2.8L); the E320 uses the M112.941 (3.2L). The W211 (2002‑2005) E320 uses the M112.955. The W211 (2005‑2008) E280 uses the M272.943 (3.0L); the E350 uses the M272.964 (3.5L). The W212 (2009‑2011) E350 uses the M272.983. The W212 (2011‑2016) E350 uses the M276.952. The engine code is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. For the M272, check the engine serial number – if it's above 2729..30, the balance shaft issue should be resolved.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair an E‑Class V6 petrol engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – M112 valve cover gaskets, M272 balance shaft wear (if caught early), M272 swirl flaps, M276 camshaft adjusters – are repairable. However, if the M272 balance shaft has failed or the engine has suffered major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes E-Class E280 / E320 / E350 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: M112/M272 no longer available new; M276 limited availability

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the E-Class V6 petrol pricing across generations. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200, with total installed cost around £1,800–£2,800. A new OEM engine replacement can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. The M276 is at the upper end of the range. On average, a full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 depending on the car model and engine type.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Class V6 petrol engine typically takes 10‑14 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Mercedes specialist). The V6 engines are more tightly packaged than the four‑cylinder models.

cta.label: → Compare E-Class V6 Petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Gear Failure (E280/E350, W211 2005–2008 & W212 2009–2011)
problems.symptoms: Check Engine Light with codes P0016 and/or P0017, rough idle that develops gradually, subtle rattle at idle, engine misfires, decreased performance. The balance shaft gear is made of improperly hardened metal and can wear out.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£3,500 [PLACEHOLDER] (balance shaft and timing gear replacement — engine‑out job).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the engine warning light appears with P0016/P0017 or you notice a rough idle, book an inspection immediately.

problems.recommendation: The M272 balance shaft gear failure is one of the most common — and most expensive if deferred — Mercedes engine repairs on 2004–2011 models. Early M272 engines (up to mid‑2006) suffer from balance shaft gear failure due to soft metal being used in the manufacturing process of the timing gear. Engines with serial numbers after 2729..30 are generally safe. If you have an early M272, check your engine serial number before buying. If you see P0016/P0017 codes, have the balance shaft inspected immediately — ignoring it can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

problems.title: M112 Valve Cover Oil Leaks (E280/E320, W210 & W211, 1998–2005)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak from the top of the engine.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: The M112 V6 is known for oil leaks from the valve cover gaskets. The valve covers use regular gaskets that can harden and leak over time. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the valve cover gaskets inspected. Replacement is relatively straightforward and much cheaper than letting the leak worsen. The M112 is the most robust of the three V6 engines.

problems.title: M276 Camshaft Adjuster Wear (E350, W212 2011–2016)
problems.symptoms: Noise on startup and during operation, rough idle, engine warning light, loss of power.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored wear can cause more extensive damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear noise on startup or notice rough running.

problems.recommendation: The most common failure item on the timing system of M276 engines is the camshaft adjuster, which produces noise on startup and during operation if worn. If you hear unusual noises or experience rough running, have the camshaft adjusters inspected. Regular oil changes (using the correct MB‑spec oil) are essential to prevent premature wear.

problems.title: M272 Intake Manifold / Swirl Flap Issues (E280/E350, W211 2005–2008)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, loss of low‑end torque, rough idle, poor fuel economy, rattling from the intake manifold.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (swirl flap linkage repair or intake manifold replacement).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection soon if warning lights appear or you hear rattling.

problems.recommendation: The M272 can suffer from swirl flap issues in the intake manifold. The swirl flaps can seize or the linkage can fail, causing engine warning lights and reduced performance. If you experience power loss or see warning lights, have the intake manifold inspected. Regular cleaning of the EGR system can help prevent this issue.

problems.title: M112/M272 Thermostat Housing & Cooling System Issues
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, sweet smell of antifreeze, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can cause head gasket failure).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you smell antifreeze or see a coolant leak, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: The thermostat housing can develop cracks or seal failures, causing coolant leaks. If you notice coolant loss or a sweet smell, have the cooling system inspected immediately. Regular coolant changes (every two years) are essential to prevent corrosion.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes E-Class E280 / E320 / E350 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — engine‑out job but cheaper than replacement
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Worth fixing — common and well‑understood repair
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes E-Class V6 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles)
* W210/W211 (M112): Valve cover gaskets replaced and no oil leaks — the M112 is the most robust of the three
* W211/W212 (M272): Engine serial number above 2729..30 — balance shaft issue resolved
* W212 (M276): Camshaft adjusters inspected and no startup noise
* A well‑maintained example — the M112 is bulletproof, the M272 is a gamble on early engines
avoidIf: * W211/W212 (M272): Any sign of P0016/P0017 codes or rough idle — balance shaft warning
* W211/W212 (M272): Engine serial number below 2729..30 — high risk of balance shaft failure
* W212 (M276): Any sign of startup noise — camshaft adjuster warning
* M112: Any sign of oil leaks from the valve covers
* Poor service history — these engines are intolerant of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes E-Class E280, E320, and E350 V6 petrol engines represent three generations of Mercedes V6 technology. The M112 (1998‑2005) is the most robust of the three — a proven SOHC 3‑valve V6 with a solid reputation for reliability. The M272 (2005‑2011) is a more modern DOHC 4‑valve V6, but early engines suffer from a well‑documented balance shaft gear failure — check the engine serial number before buying. The M276 (2011‑2016) is a direct‑injection V6 that is refined and powerful, but camshaft adjuster wear is a known concern. Buy on service history and engine serial number above all else — a well‑maintained E-Class V6 with the right engine is a rewarding car; a neglected example can be expensive to repair. As one specialist advised: "The M112 proved itself. It's children (M27x) have been similarly reliable, except for the balance shaft issue in the 2006‑2009 era".

cta.label: → Compare E-Class V6 Petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes E-Class E280 / E320 / E350 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Engine Serial Number — M272: Check if above 2729..30 (balance shaft issue resolved)
2. Cold Start — Any rattle or noise? (M276 camshaft adjuster or M272 balance shaft warning)
3. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any P0016/P0017? (M272 balance shaft)
4. Service History — Regular oil changes (every 8,000‑10,000 miles) — this is non‑negotiable
5. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve covers (M112)
6. Running Quality — Any rough idle, hesitation, or rattling? (balance shaft or swirl flap warning)
7. Coolant Level — Any sign of coolant loss? (thermostat housing warning)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
